'''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito Domain]] (now [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|Calligraphy]] of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of [[:en:Tokugawa Harutoshi|Tokugawa Harutoshi]], the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito]]. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the [[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] school established by [[:en:Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built [[:en:Kairaku-en|Kairaku-en]], a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] ([[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori]]).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>
==Family== * Father: [[:en:Tokugawa Harutoshi|Tokugawa Harutoshi]] * Mother: Toyama-dono * Wife: [[:en:Princess Yoshiko (Arisugawa-no-miya)|Arisugawa Yoshiko]] (1804–1893) **1st Son: Tokugawa Yoshiatsu (1832–1868) **2nd Son: Jiromaro (1833–1834) **5th Daughter: Mihime (1835–1835) **7th Son: [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] * Concubine: Harigawa-dono **1st Daughter: Masahime (1822–1839) **2nd Daughter: Iromotohime (1825–1826) **3rd Daughter: Iwaihime (1827–1853) married Yamanobe Yoshimasa *Concubine: Onao no Kata ** 4th Daughter: Hirohime (1834–1835) b ** 4th Son: Shiromaro (1835–1836) ** 7th Daughter: Yohime (1837–1843) ** 7th Son: Matsudaira Naoyoshi (1839–1862) ** 8th Daughter: Ichiyohime (1840–1843) ** Son: Matsudaira Takeakira (1842–1882) of [[:en:Hamada Domain|Hamada Domain]] ** 13th Son: Yosanmaro (1844–1844) *Concubine: Sadako **3rd Son: Saburomaro (1835–1837) ** 6th Daughter: Matsuhime (1836–1903) married [[:en:Nanbu Toshihisa|Nanbu Toshihisa]] ** 5th Son: Ikeda Yoshinari (1837-1877) of [[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori Domain]] ** 9th Son: Ikeda Mochimasa (1839-1899) of [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]] ** 9th Daughter: Takako (1841-1869) married [[:en:Date Yoshikuni|Date Yoshikuni]] ** 12th Son: Yonimaro (1844-1844) *Concubine: Yanagihara-dono **6th Son: Rokuromaro (1837-1838) *Concubine: Toshiko **12th Daughter: Seihime (1843-1844) **11th son: Kitsuregawa Tsunauji (1844-1874) of [[Kitsuregawa Domain]] *Concubine: Mutsuko **14th Son: Matsudaira Akikuni (1849-1864) **11th Daughter: Tokugawa Sadako (1850-1872) married [[:en:Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] **17th Son: Tsuchiya Shigenao (1854-1904) of [[:en:Tsuchiura Domain|Tsuchiura Domain]] **18th Son: [[:en:Tokugawa Akitake|Tokugawa Akitake]] **20th Son: Tatsumoro (1856-1858) **22nd Son: Matsudaira Yoriyuki (1858-1873) of [[:en:Moriyama Domain|Moriyama Domain]] *Concubine: Tokuko ** 15th Son: Yogomaro (1849-1849) ** 16th Son: [[:en:Matsudaira Tadakazu|Matsudaira Tadakazu]] ** 12th Daughter: Aihime (1852-1914) married Inoue Masayori ** 21st Son: Ichimaru (1856–1856) *Concubine: Michiko ** 13th Daughter: Hisahime (1853-1853) *Concubine: Etsuko ** 19th Son: [[:en:Matsudaira Nobunori|Matsudaira Nobunori]] ** 14th Daughter: Yasuhime (1857-1859) ** 15th Daughter: Masahime (1858–1873) ** ** ** **
== Works== [[File:徳川斉昭揮毫.jpg|thumb|Writing by Tokugawa Nariaki]] Published posthumously: * ''Kōdōkan ki'' 弘道館記 (1937). Ed. by Meiji Seitoku Kinen Gakkai 明治聖德記念學會. Tokyo: Meiji Seitoku Kinen Gakkai 明治聖德記念學會. * ''Meikun ippanshō'' 明君一斑抄 (1910–1911). Ed. by Kurokawa Mamichi 黒川真道. Tokyo: Dōbunkan 同文館.

'''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito Domain]] (now [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|Calligraphy]] of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of [[:en:Tokugawa Harutoshi|Tokugawa Harutoshi]], the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito]]. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the [[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] school established by [[:en:Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built [[:en:Kairaku-en|Kairaku-en]], a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] ([[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori]]).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>

'''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito Domain]] (now [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|Calligraphy]] of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of [[:en:Tokugawa Harutoshi|Tokugawa Harutoshi]], the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito]]. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the [[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] school established by [[:en:Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built [[:en:Kairaku-en|Kairaku-en]], a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] ([[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori]]).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>

(1837-1877) of [[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori Domain]] ** 9th Son: Ikeda Mochimasa (1839-1899) of [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]] ** 9th Daughter: Takako (1841-1869) married [[:en:Date Kitsuregawa Tsunauji (1844-1874) of [[Kitsuregawa Domain]] *Concubine: Mutsuko **14th Son: Matsudaira Akikuni (1849-1864) **11th Daughter: Tokugawa Sadako (1850-1872) married [[:en:Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] **17th Son: Tsuchiya Shigenao (1854-1904) of [[:en:Tsuchiura Domain|Tsuchiura Domain]] **18th Son: [[:en:Tokugawa Akitake|Tokugawa Akitake]] **20th Son: Tatsumoro (1856-1858) **22nd Son: Matsudaira Yoriyuki (1858-1873) of [[:en:Moriyama Domain|Moriyama Domain]] *Concubine: Tokuko ** 15th Son: Yogomaro (1849-1849) ** 16th Son: [[:en:Matsudaira Etsuko ** 19th Son: [[:en:Matsudaira Nobunori|Matsudaira Nobunori]] ** 14th Daughter: Yasuhime (1857-1859) ** 15th Daughter: Masahime (1858–1873) ** ** ** **
== Works== [[File:徳川斉昭揮毫.jpg|thumb|Writing by Tokugawa Nariaki]] Published posthumously: * ''Kōdōkan ki'' 弘道館記 (1937). Ed. by Meiji Seitoku Kinen Gakkai 明治聖德記念學會. Tokyo: Meiji Seitoku Kinen Gakkai 明治聖德記念學會. * ''Meikun ippanshō'' 明君一斑抄 (1910–1911). Ed. by Kurokawa Mamichi 黒川真道. Tokyo: Dōbunkan 同文館.

'''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito Domain]] (now [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|Calligraphy]] of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of [[:en:Tokugawa Harutoshi|Tokugawa Harutoshi]], the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito]]. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the [[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] school established by [[:en:Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built [[:en:Kairaku-en|Kairaku-en]], a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] ([[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori]]).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>

Yoshiatsu (1832–1868) **2nd Son: Jiromaro (1833–1834) **5th Daughter: Mihime (1835–1835) **7th Son: [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] * Concubine: Harigawa-dono **1st Daughter: Masahime (1822–1839) **2nd Daughter: Iromotohime (1825–1826) **3rd Daughter: Iwaihime (1827–1853) married Yamanobe Yoshimasa *Concubine: Onao no Kata ** 4th Daughter: Hirohime (1834–1835) b ** 4th Son: Shiromaro (1835–1836) ** 7th Daughter: Yohime (1837–1843) ** 7th Son: Matsudaira Naoyoshi (1839–1862) ** 8th Daughter: Ichiyohime (1840–1843) ** Son: Matsudaira Takeakira (1842–1882) of [[:en:Hamada Domain|Hamada Domain]] ** 13th Son: Yosanmaro (1844–1844) *Concubine: Sadako **3rd Son: Saburomaro (1835–1837) ** 6th Daughter: Matsuhime (1836–1903) married [[:en:Nanbu Toshihisa|Nanbu Toshihisa]] ** 5th Son: Ikeda Yoshinari (1837-1877) of [[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori Domain]] ** 9th Son: Ikeda Mochimasa (1839-1899) of [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]] ** 9th Daughter: Takako (1841-1869) married [[:en:Date Yoshikuni|Date Yoshikuni]] ** 12th Son: Yonimaro (1844-1844) *Concubine: Yanagihara-dono **6th Son: Rokuromaro (1837-1838) *Concubine: Toshiko **12th Daughter: Seihime (1843-1844) **11th son: Kitsuregawa Tsunauji (1844-1874) of [[Kitsuregawa Domain]] *Concubine: Mutsuko **14th Son: Matsudaira Akikuni (1849-1864) **11th Daughter: Tokugawa Sadako (1850-1872) married [[:en:Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] **17th Son: Tsuchiya Shigenao (1854-1904) of [[:en:Tsuchiura Domain|Tsuchiura Domain]] **18th Son: [[:en:Tokugawa Akitake|Tokugawa Akitake]] **20th Son: Tatsumoro (1856-1858) **22nd Son: Matsudaira Yoriyuki (1858-1873) of [[:en:Moriyama Domain|Moriyama Domain]] *Concubine: Tokuko ** 15th Son: Yogomaro (1849-1849) ** 16th Son: [[:en:Matsudaira Tadakazu|Matsudaira Tadakazu]] ** 12th Daughter: Aihime (1852-1914) married Inoue Masayori ** 21st Son: Ichimaru (1856–1856) *Concubine: Michiko ** 13th Daughter: Hisahime (1853-1853) *Concubine: Etsuko ** 19th Son: [[:en:Matsudaira Nobunori|Matsudaira Nobunori]] ** 14th Daughter: Yasuhime (1857-1859) ** 15th Daughter: Masahime (1858–1873) ** ** ** **
== Works== [[File:徳川斉昭揮毫.jpg|thumb|Writing by Tokugawa Nariaki]] Published posthumously: * ''Kōdōkan ki'' 弘道館記 (1937). Ed. by Meiji Seitoku Kinen Gakkai 明治聖德記念學會. Tokyo: Meiji Seitoku Kinen Gakkai 明治聖德記念學會. * ''Meikun ippanshō'' 明君一斑抄 (1910–1911). Ed. by Kurokawa Mamichi 黒川真道. Tokyo: Dōbunkan 同文館.

'''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito Domain]] (now [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|Calligraphy]] of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of [[:en:Tokugawa Harutoshi|Tokugawa Harutoshi]], the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito]]. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the [[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] school established by [[:en:Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built [[:en:Kairaku-en|Kairaku-en]], a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] ([[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori]]).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>

'''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito Domain]] (now [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|Calligraphy]] of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of [[:en:Tokugawa Harutoshi|Tokugawa Harutoshi]], the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito]]. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the [[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] school established by [[:en:Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built [[:en:Kairaku-en|Kairaku-en]], a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] ([[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori]]).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>

'''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito Domain]] (now [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|Calligraphy]] of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of [[:en:Tokugawa Harutoshi|Tokugawa Harutoshi]], the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito]]. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the [[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] school established by [[:en:Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built [[:en:Kairaku-en|Kairaku-en]], a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] ([[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori]]).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>

Yoshiatsu (1832–1868) **2nd Son: Jiromaro (1833–1834) **5th Daughter: Mihime (1835–1835) **7th Son: [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] * Concubine: Harigawa-dono **1st Daughter: Masahime (1822–1839) **2nd Daughter: Iromotohime (1825–1826) **3rd Daughter: Iwaihime (1827–1853) married Yamanobe Yoshimasa *Concubine: Onao no Kata ** 4th Daughter: Hirohime (1834–1835) b ** 4th Son: Shiromaro (1835–1836) ** 7th Daughter: Yohime (1837–1843) ** 7th Son: Matsudaira Naoyoshi (1839–1862) ** 8th Daughter: Ichiyohime (1840–1843) ** Son: Matsudaira Takeakira (1842–1882) of [[:en:Hamada Domain|Hamada Domain]] ** 13th Son: Yosanmaro (1844–1844) *Concubine: Sadako **3rd Son: Saburomaro (1835–1837) ** 6th Daughter: Matsuhime (1836–1903) married [[:en:Nanbu Toshihisa|Nanbu Toshihisa]] ** 5th Son: Ikeda Yoshinari (1837-1877) of [[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori Domain]] ** 9th Son: Ikeda Mochimasa (1839-1899) of [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]] ** 9th Daughter: Takako (1841-1869) married [[:en:Date Yoshikuni|Date Yoshikuni]] ** 12th Son: Yonimaro (1844-1844) *Concubine: Yanagihara-dono **6th Son: Rokuromaro (1837-1838) *Concubine: Toshiko **12th Daughter: Seihime (1843-1844) **11th son: Kitsuregawa Tsunauji (1844-1874) of [[Kitsuregawa Domain]] *Concubine: Mutsuko **14th Son: Matsudaira Akikuni (1849-1864) **11th Daughter: Tokugawa Sadako (1850-1872) married [[:en:Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] **17th Son: Tsuchiya Shigenao (1854-1904) of [[:en:Tsuchiura Domain|Tsuchiura Domain]] **18th Son: [[:en:Tokugawa Akitake|Tokugawa Akitake]] **20th Son: Tatsumoro (1856-1858) **22nd Son: Matsudaira Yoriyuki (1858-1873) of [[:en:Moriyama Domain|Moriyama Domain]] *Concubine: Tokuko ** 15th Son: Yogomaro (1849-1849) ** 16th Son: [[:en:Matsudaira Tadakazu|Matsudaira Tadakazu]] ** 12th Daughter: Aihime (1852-1914) married Inoue Masayori ** 21st Son: Ichimaru (1856–1856) *Concubine: Michiko ** 13th Daughter: Hisahime (1853-1853) *Concubine: Etsuko ** 19th Son: [[:en:Matsudaira Nobunori|Matsudaira Nobunori]] ** 14th Daughter: Yasuhime (1857-1859) ** 15th Daughter: Masahime (1858–1873) ** ** ** **
== Works== [[File:徳川斉昭揮毫.jpg|thumb|Writing by Tokugawa Nariaki]] Published posthumously: * ''Kōdōkan ki'' 弘道館記 (1937). Ed. by Meiji Seitoku Kinen Gakkai 明治聖德記念學會. Tokyo: Meiji Seitoku Kinen Gakkai 明治聖德記念學會. * ''Meikun ippanshō'' 明君一斑抄 (1910–1911). Ed. by Kurokawa Mamichi 黒川真道. Tokyo: Dōbunkan 同文館.

'''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito Domain]] (now [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|Calligraphy]] of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of [[:en:Tokugawa Harutoshi|Tokugawa Harutoshi]], the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Mito Domain|Mito]]. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the [[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] school established by [[:en:Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built [[:en:Kairaku-en|Kairaku-en]], a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] ([[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori]]).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>

'''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito Domain}} (now {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}}) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = {{ill|Japanese calligraphy|en|Japanese calligraphy|WD=Q660086|lt=Calligraphy}} of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of {{ill|Tokugawa Harutoshi|en|Tokugawa Harutoshi|WD=Q728162|lt=Tokugawa Harutoshi}}, the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito}}. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with {{ill|Ii Naosuke|en|Ii Naosuke|WD=Q716940|lt=Ii Naosuke}} on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the {{ill|Mitogaku|en|Mitogaku|WD=Q260529|lt=Mitogaku}} school established by {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|en|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|WD=Q1194944|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}}. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}.<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built {{ill|Kairaku-en|en|Kairaku-en|WD=Q71952|lt=Kairaku-en}}, a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} ({{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|WD=Q905521|lt=Tottori}}).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>

Hisahime (1853-1853) *Concubine: Etsuko ** 19th Son: {{ill|Matsudaira Nobunori|en|Matsudaira Nobunori|WD=Q930282|lt=Matsudaira Nobunori}} ** 14th Daughter: Yasuhime (1857-1859) ** 15th Daughter: '''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito Domain}} (now {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}}) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = {{ill|Japanese calligraphy|en|Japanese calligraphy|WD=Q660086|lt=Calligraphy}} of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of {{ill|Tokugawa Harutoshi|en|Tokugawa Harutoshi|WD=Q728162|lt=Tokugawa Harutoshi}}, the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito}}. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with {{ill|Ii Naosuke|en|Ii Naosuke|WD=Q716940|lt=Ii Naosuke}} on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the {{ill|Mitogaku|en|Mitogaku|WD=Q260529|lt=Mitogaku}} school established by {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|en|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|WD=Q1194944|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}}. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}.<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built {{ill|Kairaku-en|en|Kairaku-en|WD=Q71952|lt=Kairaku-en}}, a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} ({{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|WD=Q905521|lt=Tottori}}).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>

(Arisugawa-no-miya)|WD=Q8944971|lt=Arisugawa Yoshiko}} (1804–1893) **1st Son: Tokugawa Yoshiatsu (1832–1868) **2nd Son: Jiromaro (1833–1834) **5th Daughter: Mihime (1835–1835) **7th Son: [[Tokugawa Kata ** 4th Daughter: Hirohime (1834–1835) b ** 4th Son: Shiromaro (1835–1836) ** 7th Daughter: Yohime (1837–1843) ** 7th Son: Matsudaira Naoyoshi (1839–1862) ** 8th Daughter: Ichiyohime (1840–1843) Masahime (1858–1873) ** ** ** **
== Works== [[File:徳川斉昭揮毫.jpg|thumb|Writing by Tokugawa Nariaki]] Published posthumously: * ''Kōdōkan ki'' 弘道館記 (1937). Ed. by Meiji Seitoku Kinen Gakkai 明治聖德記念學會. Tokyo: Meiji Seitoku Kinen Gakkai 明治聖德記念學會. * ''Meikun ippanshō'' 明君一斑抄 (1910–1911). Ed. by Kurokawa Mamichi 黒川真道. Tokyo: Dōbunkan 同文館.

{{ill|Matsudaira Yorikata|WD=Q6638084}}; {{ill|Tokugawa Jūko|WD=Q6997513}}; {{ill|Tokugawa Narinobu|WD=Q6998088}}|native_name=徳川斉昭|given_name={{ill|Nariaki|WD=Q123636017}}|resting_place={{ill|Zuiryūsan|WD=Q11573978}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1323770]] Wikidata name: Tokugawa Nariaki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Japanese daimyo}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Tokugawa Nariaki | image = Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | caption = | order = 9th {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Lord of Mito}} | term_start = 1829 | term_end = 1844 | predecessor = [[Tokugawa Narinobu]] | successor = [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] | birth_date = {{birth date|1800|4|4|mf=y}} | birth_place = | death_date = {{death date and age|1860|9|29|1800|4|4|mf=y}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | father = {{ill|Tokugawa Harutoshi|en|Tokugawa Harutoshi|WD=Q728162|lt=Tokugawa Harutoshi}} | mother = Toyama-dono | spouse = {{ill|Princess Yoshiko (Arisugawa-no-miya)|en|Princess Yoshiko (Arisugawa-no-miya)|WD=Q8944971|lt=Arisugawa Yoshiko}} (1804–1893) | children = {{plain list| * several, including * [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] * {{ill|Tokugawa Akitake|en|Tokugawa Akitake|WD=Q3530485|lt=Tokugawa Akitake}} * {{ill|Matsudaira Tadakazu|en|Matsudaira Tadakazu|WD=Q6788070|lt=Matsudaira Tadakazu}} * {{ill|Matsudaira Nobunori|en|Matsudaira Nobunori|WD=Q930282|lt=Matsudaira Nobunori}} }} | native_name = 徳川 斉昭 | native_name_lang = ja }}
'''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito Domain}} (now {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}}) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = {{ill|Japanese calligraphy|en|Japanese calligraphy|WD=Q660086|lt=Calligraphy}} of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of {{ill|Tokugawa Harutoshi|en|Tokugawa Harutoshi|WD=Q728162|lt=Tokugawa Harutoshi}}, the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito}}. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with {{ill|Ii Naosuke|en|Ii Naosuke|WD=Q716940|lt=Ii Naosuke}} on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the {{ill|Mitogaku|en|Mitogaku|WD=Q260529|lt=Mitogaku}} school established by {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|en|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|WD=Q1194944|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}}. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}.<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built {{ill|Kairaku-en|en|Kairaku-en|WD=Q71952|lt=Kairaku-en}}, a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} ({{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|WD=Q905521|lt=Tottori}}).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>

(Arisugawa-no-miya)|WD=Q8944971|lt=Arisugawa Yoshiko}} (1804–1893) **1st Son: Tokugawa Yoshiatsu (1832–1868) **2nd Son: Jiromaro (1833–1834) **5th Daughter: Mihime (1835–1835) **7th Son: [[Tokugawa Hisahime (1853-1853) *Concubine: Etsuko ** 19th Son: {{ill|Matsudaira Nobunori|en|Matsudaira Nobunori|WD=Q930282|lt=Matsudaira Nobunori}} ** 14th Daughter: Yasuhime (1857-1859) ** 15th Daughter: Masahime (1858–1873) ** ** ** **
== Works== [[File:徳川斉昭揮毫.jpg|thumb|Writing by Tokugawa Nariaki]] Published posthumously: * ''Kōdōkan ki'' 弘道館記 (1937). Ed. by Meiji Seitoku Kinen Gakkai 明治聖德記念學會. Tokyo: Meiji Seitoku Kinen Gakkai 明治聖德記念學會. * ''Meikun ippanshō'' 明君一斑抄 (1910–1911). Ed. by Kurokawa Mamichi 黒川真道. Tokyo: Dōbunkan 同文館.

'''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito Domain}} (now {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}}) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = {{ill|Japanese calligraphy|en|Japanese calligraphy|WD=Q660086|lt=Calligraphy}} of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of {{ill|Tokugawa Harutoshi|en|Tokugawa Harutoshi|WD=Q728162|lt=Tokugawa Harutoshi}}, the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito}}. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with {{ill|Ii Naosuke|en|Ii Naosuke|WD=Q716940|lt=Ii Naosuke}} on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the {{ill|Mitogaku|en|Mitogaku|WD=Q260529|lt=Mitogaku}} school established by {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|en|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|WD=Q1194944|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}}. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}.<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built {{ill|Kairaku-en|en|Kairaku-en|WD=Q71952|lt=Kairaku-en}}, a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} ({{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|WD=Q905521|lt=Tottori}}).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>

'''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito Domain}} (now {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}}) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = {{ill|Japanese calligraphy|en|Japanese calligraphy|WD=Q660086|lt=Calligraphy}} of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of {{ill|Tokugawa Harutoshi|en|Tokugawa Harutoshi|WD=Q728162|lt=Tokugawa Harutoshi}}, the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito}}. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with {{ill|Ii Naosuke|en|Ii Naosuke|WD=Q716940|lt=Ii Naosuke}} on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the {{ill|Mitogaku|en|Mitogaku|WD=Q260529|lt=Mitogaku}} school established by {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|en|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|WD=Q1194944|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}}. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}.<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built {{ill|Kairaku-en|en|Kairaku-en|WD=Q71952|lt=Kairaku-en}}, a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} ({{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|WD=Q905521|lt=Tottori}}).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>

'''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito Domain}} (now {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}}) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = {{ill|Japanese calligraphy|en|Japanese calligraphy|WD=Q660086|lt=Calligraphy}} of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of {{ill|Tokugawa Harutoshi|en|Tokugawa Harutoshi|WD=Q728162|lt=Tokugawa Harutoshi}}, the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito}}. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with {{ill|Ii Naosuke|en|Ii Naosuke|WD=Q716940|lt=Ii Naosuke}} on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the {{ill|Mitogaku|en|Mitogaku|WD=Q260529|lt=Mitogaku}} school established by {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|en|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|WD=Q1194944|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}}. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}.<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built {{ill|Kairaku-en|en|Kairaku-en|WD=Q71952|lt=Kairaku-en}}, a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} ({{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|WD=Q905521|lt=Tottori}}).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>
==Family== * Father: {{ill|Tokugawa Harutoshi|en|Tokugawa Harutoshi|WD=Q728162|lt=Tokugawa Harutoshi}} * Mother: Toyama-dono * Wife: {{ill|Princess Yoshiko (Arisugawa-no-miya)|en|Princess Yoshiko (Arisugawa-no-miya)|WD=Q8944971|lt=Arisugawa Yoshiko}} (1804–1893) **1st Son: Tokugawa Yoshiatsu (1832–1868) **2nd Son: Jiromaro (1833–1834) **5th Daughter: Mihime (1835–1835) **7th Son: [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] * Concubine: Harigawa-dono **1st Daughter: Masahime (1822–1839) **2nd Daughter: Iromotohime (1825–1826) **3rd Daughter: Iwaihime (1827–1853) married Yamanobe Yoshimasa *Concubine: Onao no Kata ** 4th Daughter: Hirohime (1834–1835) b ** 4th Son: Shiromaro (1835–1836) ** 7th Daughter: Yohime (1837–1843) ** 7th Son: Matsudaira Naoyoshi (1839–1862) ** 8th Daughter: Ichiyohime (1840–1843) ** Son: Matsudaira Takeakira (1842–1882) of {{ill|Hamada Domain|en|Hamada Domain|WD=Q3034439|lt=Hamada Domain}} ** 13th Son: Yosanmaro (1844–1844) *Concubine: Sadako **3rd Son: Saburomaro (1835–1837) ** 6th Daughter: Matsuhime (1836–1903) married {{ill|Nanbu Toshihisa|en|Nanbu Toshihisa|WD=Q8513991|lt=Nanbu Toshihisa}} ** 5th Son: Ikeda Yoshinari (1837-1877) of {{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|WD=Q905521|lt=Tottori Domain}} ** 9th Son: Ikeda Mochimasa (1839-1899) of {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|Okayama Domain|WD=Q903497|lt=Okayama Domain}} ** 9th Daughter: Takako (1841-1869) married Daughter: Seihime (1843-1844) **11th son: Kitsuregawa Tsunauji (1844-1874) of [[Kitsuregawa Domain]] *Concubine: Mutsuko **14th Son: Matsudaira Akikuni (1849-1864) **11th Daughter: Tokugawa Sadako (1850-1872) married {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|en|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|WD=Q668338|lt=Prince Arisugawa Taruhito}} **17th Son: Tsuchiya Shigenao (1854-1904) of {{ill|Tsuchiura Domain|en|Tsuchiura Domain|WD=Q10863434|lt=Tsuchiura Domain}} **18th Son: {{ill|Tokugawa Akitake|en|Tokugawa Akitake|WD=Q3530485|lt=Tokugawa Akitake}} **20th Son: Tatsumoro (1856-1858) **22nd Son: Tadakazu|en|Matsudaira Tadakazu|WD=Q6788070|lt=Matsudaira Tadakazu}} ** 12th Daughter: Aihime (1852-1914) married Inoue Masayori ** 21st Son: Ichimaru (1856–1856) *Concubine: Michiko ** 13th Daughter: Hisahime (1853-1853) *Concubine: Etsuko ** 19th Son: {{ill|Matsudaira Nobunori|en|Matsudaira Nobunori|WD=Q930282|lt=Matsudaira Nobunori}} ** 14th Daughter: Yasuhime (1857-1859) ** 15th Daughter: Masahime (1858–1873) ** ** ** **
== Works== [[File:徳川斉昭揮毫.jpg|thumb|Writing by Tokugawa Nariaki]] Published posthumously: * ''Kōdōkan ki'' 弘道館記 (1937). Ed. by Meiji Seitoku Kinen Gakkai 明治聖德記念學會. Tokyo: Meiji Seitoku Kinen Gakkai 明治聖德記念學會. * ''Meikun ippanshō'' 明君一斑抄 (1910–1911). Ed. by Kurokawa Mamichi 黒川真道. Tokyo: Dōbunkan 同文館.

{{ill|Matsudaira Yorikata|WD=Q6638084}}; {{ill|Tokugawa Jūko|WD=Q6997513}}; {{ill|Tokugawa Narinobu|WD=Q6998088}}|native_name=徳川斉昭|given_name={{ill|Nariaki|WD=Q123636017}}|resting_place={{ill|Zuiryūsan|WD=Q11573978}}}} {{short description|Japanese daimyo}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Tokugawa Nariaki | image = Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | caption = | order = 9th {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Lord of Mito}} | term_start = 1829 | term_end = 1844 | predecessor = [[Tokugawa Narinobu]] | successor = [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] | birth_date = {{birth date|1800|4|4|mf=y}} | birth_place = | death_date = {{death date and age|1860|9|29|1800|4|4|mf=y}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | father = {{ill|Tokugawa Harutoshi|en|Tokugawa Harutoshi|WD=Q728162|lt=Tokugawa Harutoshi}} | mother = Toyama-dono | spouse = {{ill|Princess Yoshiko (Arisugawa-no-miya)|en|Princess Yoshiko (Arisugawa-no-miya)|WD=Q8944971|lt=Arisugawa Yoshiko}} (1804–1893) | children = {{plain list| * several, including * [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] * {{ill|Tokugawa Akitake|en|Tokugawa Akitake|WD=Q3530485|lt=Tokugawa Akitake}} * {{ill|Matsudaira Tadakazu|en|Matsudaira Tadakazu|WD=Q6788070|lt=Matsudaira Tadakazu}} * {{ill|Matsudaira Nobunori|en|Matsudaira Nobunori|WD=Q930282|lt=Matsudaira Nobunori}} }} | native_name = 徳川 斉昭 | native_name_lang = ja }}
'''Tokugawa Nariaki''' (徳川 斉昭, April 4, 1800 – September 29, 1860) was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who ruled the {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito Domain}} (now {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}}) and contributed to the rise of nationalism and the [[Meiji Restoration]].
== Biography == {{multiple image | align = right | direction = horizontal | width = | image1 = WLA lacma Flower Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width1 = 100 | alt1 = "Flower" | caption1 = | image2 = WLA lacma Moon Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width2 = 100 | alt2 = "Moon" | caption2 = | image3 = WLA lacma Snow Calligraphy by Lord Tokugawa Nariaki.jpg | width3 = 100 | alt3 = "Snow" | caption3 = | footer = {{ill|Japanese calligraphy|en|Japanese calligraphy|WD=Q660086|lt=Calligraphy}} of (L-to-R) "flower", "moon", and "snow" by Tokugawa Nariaki }}
=== Clan leader === Nariaki was the 3rd son of {{ill|Tokugawa Harutoshi|en|Tokugawa Harutoshi|WD=Q728162|lt=Tokugawa Harutoshi}}, the seventh-generation ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito}}. The family headship first passed to Harutoshi's eldest son Narinobu, before being passed on to Nariaki in 1829.<ref>[http://www.geocities.jp/bane2161/tokugawanariaki.html 徳川斉昭]</ref> Nariaki was also leader of the ''[[Sonnō jōi|Jōi]]'' (expel the barbarian) party and made a Bakufu adviser on national defence.<ref>McOmie, William ''The Opening of Japan, 1853–1855'' (Folkestone, Global Oriental, 2006), p. 138</ref> His childhood name was Torasaburo (虎三郎) later changed to Keisaburo (敬三郎).
=== Bakufu official === Nariaki was put in charge of {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} efforts to defend the country against encroaching foreigners. His own view was that the bakufu should strengthen its military and fight the foreigners, and was at odds with {{ill|Ii Naosuke|en|Ii Naosuke|WD=Q716940|lt=Ii Naosuke}} on the issue. He was pro-emperor and favored imperial restoration. Nariaki also greatly expanded the {{ill|Mitogaku|en|Mitogaku|WD=Q260529|lt=Mitogaku}} school established by {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|en|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|WD=Q1194944|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}}. He wrote a document entitled "Japan, Reject the Westerners" in 1853. in this document, he stated ten reasons why Japan should stay isolated from the rest of the world. He said that the Japanese people had a choice between war and peace, but clearly to him, the Japanese people should choose war so that Westerners would not intrude into Japan's affairs.
Despite his resistance to Westernization, Nariaki was significantly influenced by the {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} school.<ref name=Josephson>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=125, 128|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref> [[Ōkuni Takamasa]], a student of [[Hirata Atsutane]] attempted to persuade him to combine ritual with technology in order to protect Japan's borders and expand Japan as an empire. This depended on reinvigorating the Japanese "national spirit".<ref name=Josephson/> Ōkuni and Nariaki therefore laid some foundations for the Meiji restoration as well as the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}.<ref name=Josephson/>
Nariaki and Naosuke fought over who would succeed the Shōgun Iesada, with Nariaki championing his son [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]]. Naosuke, who eventually prevailed, favored the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] daimyo [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Tokugawa Yoshitomi]].
=== Legacy === In 1841, Nariaki built {{ill|Kairaku-en|en|Kairaku-en|WD=Q71952|lt=Kairaku-en}}, a garden whose fame lasts to this day.
Nariaki retired in 1844 in favor of his son [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu|Yoshiatsu]], and died of a heart attack in 1860, at age 60.
Three of the leading figures of the 1860s were in fact natural brothers, all being sons of Nariaki: Hitotsubashi Yoshinobu, who became the 15th and last shōgun as [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] in 1866; [[Tokugawa Yoshiatsu]] of Mito; and [[Ikeda Yoshinori]] of {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} ({{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|WD=Q905521|lt=Tottori}}).<ref>Beasley, William. (1955). ''Select Documents on Japanese Foreign Policy, 1853–1868''. p. 11 n3.</ref>
